title: Knotted pile rug: the Turkish knot
subtitle: How to set up a rug warp and tie Turkish knots to build a dense, patterned pile rug
excerpt: The Turkish knot (also called the Ghiordes knot) is the pile knot used in traditional hand-knotted rugs across Turkey, Persia, and the Caucasus. This entry covers warping a simple frame loom for rug work, tying the knot correctly, weaving the ground picks between knot rows, and finishing the pile. The technique is the foundation of the flat-woven rug tradition.
sourceNotes: Turkish knot (Ghiordes knot) rug technique synthesised from general hand-knotted rug references. The technique is documented in Marla Mallett, Woven Structures (1998) and general flat-weave rug literature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A symmetric pile knot (also called Ghiordes knot) tied around two adjacent warp ends by looping a cut pile length over both ends so both tails come up between the pair and outside each end. The most common knot in hand-knotted rug weaving.

The number of knots per square centimetre of rug surface. Higher knot density requires a closer warp sett and finer pile yarn, producing a more detailed pattern. Typical hand-knotted rugs range from 4 to over 30 knots per square centimetre.
A weft pass of plain weave woven between each row of knots to lock the knots in place and give the rug its structural integrity. Usually 2-4 ground picks are woven between each knot row.

The direction in which the cut pile yarn naturally leans after knotting, determined by the direction from which the rug was woven. Pile direction affects the colour appearance: looking with the pile gives a different colour impression than looking against it.

content.text: The
Turkish knot
is a symmetric knot: both tails come up on opposite sides of a two-warp-end pair, giving a more stable and upright pile than the asymmetric Persian knot. The
knot density
is controlled by the warp sett and the knot-tying speed: a closer sett and more knots per row produce a finer pattern. The
ground picks
between knot rows lock the knots and prevent the pile from shifting in the finished rug. Once cut, the
pile direction
becomes fixed and the rug should be laid so the pile leans away from the main light source, giving the deepest colour rendition.
Method
Step 1: Set up the rug warp

Use a strong, smooth warp yarn (linen or cotton at 8-10 ends per cm) on a rigid frame or simple floor loom. The warp must be under firm, even tension: a slack warp produces uneven knot rows and a distorted rug. For a first sampler, work 20 cm wide at 8 ends per cm (160 ends total). Wind the warp and secure it to the frame at firm tension. Weave 2-3 cm of plain weave at the base with a thick weft yarn to stabilise the warp before beginning the knot rows.
Step 2: Cut the pile yarn

Cut pile yarn lengths at 2.5 times the desired pile height plus 2 cm: for a 15 mm pile height, cut lengths of approximately 4 cm. Use a cardboard gauge at the correct width and cut multiple lengths at once by wrapping yarn around the gauge and slicing one edge. Prepare enough lengths in each colour for the full pattern before sitting at the loom: interrupting knotting to cut more yarn breaks the rhythm of a colour sequence.
Step 3: Tie the Turkish knots

Working from left to right across the warp, take one cut pile length and hold it horizontally behind a pair of adjacent warp ends. Bring the left tail up and over the left warp end, then down between the two ends. Bring the right tail up and over the right warp end, then down between the two ends. Both tails hang forward between the pair. Pull both tails firmly to seat the knot against the previous row. Continue across the warp, following the colour pattern. One complete row of knots across a 20 cm warp (80 pairs) takes 10-15 minutes when the rhythm is established.
Step 4: Weave the ground picks

After each knot row, weave 2-4 ground picks of plain weave, beating each pick firmly with the beater or a fork-style beater. The ground picks should be barely visible beneath the pile: they are structural, not decorative. Beat firmly enough that the ground picks compress the knots and hold them firmly in place but not so hard that the warp starts to distort from its correct width.
Step 5: Cut and trim the pile

After weaving several knot rows, trim the pile to an even height using sharp scissors. Hold the scissors flat against the pile surface and cut across the whole width. A consistent pile height reads more clearly as pattern than an uneven one. The pile can also be cut at angles to add shading within the pattern areas: a bevel cut at the colour boundaries between motifs makes the edges between colours sharper and better defined.

items.problem: The knots pull out when the ground picks are beaten
items.cause: The knots were not seated firmly against the previous ground row
items.fix: Pull each knot down firmly against the ground picks before moving to the next pair. The knot should not slide when you pull both tails upward gently after tying.

items.problem: The rug is narrowing as it grows: the warp is pulling inward
items.cause: The ground weft is too tight and is drawing the selvedge warp ends inward
items.fix: Let the ground weft arc across the shed before beating and check the warp width after every 3-4 knot rows. The weaving width should match the starting warp width throughout.

items.problem: The pattern is illegible: the colour areas bleed into each other
items.cause: The knot density is too low relative to the fineness of the pattern design
items.fix: Increase the warp sett to allow more knots per centimetre, or simplify the pattern design to use larger, bolder shapes. Patterns with motifs smaller than 3 knots across are very difficult to read at 8 ends per cm.
